payload.biographical_sketch: Renaissance philosopher (1463-1494), wrote Oration on the Dignity of Man.

On 2. this interpretation, Pico champions the human being as a powerful magus 3. who, released upon the world, can act upon it with an array of mysterious 4. magical and occult powers. One commentator has argued that “the com- 5. plete Renaissance Magus, as he burst upon the world for the first time 6. in Pico’s oration in his full power and Dignity, was a practitioner of both 7. natural magic and also of its ‘supreme form,’ practical Cabala,” and the 8. “oration was to echo and re-echo throughout the Renaissance, and it is, 9. indeed, the great charter of Renaissance magic.”126 This occultist account 10. of the Oratio, however, has perhaps been the most controversial approach 11. to Pico’s thought and has been the target of historical criticism.127 12. 13. Alongside these varied interpretations of the Oratio, which look back to 14. find antecedents, sources, and influences for Pico’s views on the human 15. condition, are some approaches that find in them seminal anticipations 16. of future historical movements and ideas. For instance, the fact that some 17. of Pico’s interests in the Oratio are theological, along with the fact that Pico 18. initially experienced difficulties at the hands of Roman church authori- 19. ties, has led a few commentators to portray Pico as an harbinger of the 20. Protestant Reformation and even to claim that Pico’s articulation of his 21. theses was nothing less than an unsuccessful version of Martin Luther’s 22. famous episode at Wittenberg.128 Other, arguably less careful, placements 23. of Pico in the history of ideas present him as an early Nietzschean philoso- 24. pher of will or a Sartrean existentialist.129 25. 26. In light of the varied and often conflicting interpretations of Pico’s 27. views, a few general observations can be drawn. I contend that the 28. 29. 126 Frances A. This volume provides a comprehensive presentation of the philosophical 22. work of the fifteenth-century Renaissance thinker Giovanni Pico della Miran- 23. dola. In essays specially commissioned for this book, a distinguished group 24. of scholars presents the central topics and texts of Pico’s literary output. Best 25. known as the author of the celebrated “Oration on the Dignity of Man,” a 26. magnificent speech originally intended to introduce a debate of 900 theses 27. to be held in Rome before the pope, the College of Cardinals, and an interna- 28. tional group of scholars, Pico also wrote several other prominent works. They 29. include an influential diatribe against astrology, an ambitious metaphysical 30. treatise attempting to reconcile Platonic and Aristotelian metaphysical views, 31. and writings on a range of subjects such as magic, Kabbalah, the church, the 32. philosophy of religion, and the philosophy of knowledge. The first volume of 33. its kind in English, this collection of essays will be of value not only to advanced 34. students and specialists of late medieval and Renaissance thought but also to 35. those interested in Italian humanism and Renaissance Aristotelianism and 36. Neoplatonism. 37. 38. M. V. Dougherty is assistant professor of philosophy at Ohio Dominican Uni- 39. versity. His research in the history of philosophy includes work on the think- 40. ing of Thomas Aquinas, Aristotle, and René Descartes as well as Pico della 41. Mirandola. 42. 43. i 44. 45. 46. 47. Universities of Padua, Bologna, and Paris, are established facts. On 30. the other hand Pico’s most important composition, in which he fully 31. expressed his competence in scholastic philosophy and theology, i.e, the 32. Apologia, so it seems, has been less frequently discussed in comparison to 33. any other of his works. In other words, our view of the relation between 34. Pico and the scholastics should be based first of all upon a detailed 35. analysis of the Apologia and its background and sources—but there is as 36. yet no such analysis. At the centre of this chapter, then, I shall introduce 37. an analysis of the main themes and aims of the Apologia, and a detailed 38. analysis of a number of theses, in order to put this work in the context 39. of the theological crisis of the age. 40. 41. I have already discussed some of the differences between Ficino’s De 42. 43. Christiana religione and Pico’s Apologia. Let me now put Pico’s book in 44. the wider context of this study. This is a work of technical theology. 45. Although Pico is not a professional theologian like Caroli, his Apologia 46. 47. is far more technical than Caroli’s Liber dierum lucensium. This can be 48. explained by the differences in background and literary form. Caroli is 49. writing a dialogue concerning one aspect of the crisis, the institutional 50. aspect. Introduction 5 5. 6. a significant number of new editions of Pico’s works have appeared, at 7. times supplemented with modern translations, and they collectively tes- 8. tify to the growing interest in Pico’s work.18 This increase in editions 9. and translations has been matched by a rise in the amount and qual- 10. ity of scholarly commentary on Pico’s literary corpus. The study of this 11. secondary literature on Pico has been greatly enhanced with the recent 12. appearance of substantive bibliographies that reliably catalogue the sec- 13. ondary literature on Pico along with the printed editions and translations 14. of Pico’s works,19 and reference works like these join the other standard 15. tools at the disposal of the contemporary student of Pico.20 Special men- 16. tion should be made also of significant collaborative projects in Pico 17. studies. Proceedings from conferences marking the fifth centenaries of 18. Pico’s birth and death featured essays from prominent historians of the 19. Renaissance period.21 Further, in addition to online electronic editions 20. of Pico’s Oratio and the 900 Theses with an accompanying collaborative 21. commentary, hosted by Brown University and the University of Bologna, 22. there has appeared a series dedicated to editing and translating the 23. volumes composing Pico’s Kabbalistic library collection.22 Additionally, 24. 25. 18 Of special interest may be the forthcoming bilingual publication of Pico’s Oratio, 900 26. Theses, Apologia, and Letters in several volumes of the I Tatti Renaissance Library series 27. from Harvard University Press. 28. 29. 19 Pichiana: Bibliografia delle edizioni e degli studi, ed. Leonardo Quaquarelli and Zita Zanardi 30. (Florence: Leo S. Olschki, 2005). The last portion of this volume (pages 335–410) consists 31. of a bibliography of secondary literature and has been reprinted with the identification 32. of the co-editors. See Leonardo Quaquarelli and M. V. Dougherty, Bibliografia XIX e XX 33. Secolo (Florence: Leo S. Olschki, 2007). For a recent study on Pico see Louis 2. 3. 4. 5. giovanni pico della mirandola and his theological method 281 6. 7. as the humanist who had the best scholastic education or training, and 8. both his famous enthusiasm for these thinkers and their style, as well 9. as his formal education, which included, among others, studies in the 10. Universities of Padua, Bologna, and Paris, are established facts. On 11. the other hand Pico’s most important composition, in which he fully 12. expressed his competence in scholastic philosophy and theology, i.e, the 13. Apologia, so it seems, has been less frequently discussed in comparison to 14. any other of his works. In other words, our view of the relation between 15. Pico and the scholastics should be based first of all upon a detailed 16. analysis of the Apologia and its background and sources—but there is as 17. yet no such analysis. At the centre of this chapter, then, I shall introduce 18. an analysis of the main themes and aims of the Apologia, and a detailed 19. analysis of a number of theses, in order to put this work in the context 20. of the theological crisis of the age. 21. 22. I have already discussed some of the differences between Ficino’s De 23. 24. Christiana religione and Pico’s Apologia. Let me now put Pico’s book in 25. the wider context of this study. This is a work of technical theology. 26. Although Pico is not a professional theologian like Caroli, his Apologia 27. 28. is far more technical than Caroli’s Liber dierum lucensium. This can be 29. explained by the differences in background and literary form. Caroli is 30. writing a dialogue concerning one aspect of the crisis, the institutional 31. aspect. He is concerned with the problem of institutional authority and 32. with an institutional reform of his monastery and order. In Pico’s work 33. it is the problem of authority in matters of faith which stands at the 34. centre of the discussion. Caroli need not justify his religious positions, 35. since his book does not deal technically with questions of faith and 36. heresy. Pico deals especially with such questions, and his work is an 37. answer to people who are well trained in dogmatic theology. This, 38. together with Pico’s recent scholastic studies in Paris, would explain 39. the frequent and detailed discussions of scholastic texts and opinions in 40. his work. But this should not mislead us. Pico’s book is not yet another 41. work of scholastic theology. Indeed, his aim is to question the very basis 42. of scholastic theology and the assumption made by some contemporary 43. theologians that some of the major figures in scholastic theology should 44. be regarded as sources of authority in matters of faith comparable to 45. Scripture and the Creed. In a way, one could suggest that what Caroli 46. 47. Valcke, Pic de la Mirandole—un itinéraire philosophique (Paris 2005); see, on pp. 451–465, a 48. recent bibliography. I shall refer to other relevant discussions of Pico below. 49. 50. 51. 52. 282 chapter four 53. 54. wishes to see happening in the Church from the institutional point of 55. view is not unlike what Pico wishes to accomplish on the dogmatic and 56. philosophical side. 57. 58. A comparison between Ficino’s and Pico’s books has already been 59. drawn at the end of the last chapter. I shall only emphasize again 60. that on the issue of authority in matters of philosophy and theology 61. their positions are not all that different. Chapter 1 2. 3. Scientia naturalis, Kabbalah and Celestial 4. Spheres: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola on 5. Astrology (1486–1493) 6. 7. 1 Introduction 8. 9. The development of the astrological ideas of Giovanni Pico della Mirandola 10. remains one of the most intriguing aspects of his legacy. Although Pico only 11. analysed astrology in depth in the last treatise, the Disputationes adversus astr- 12. ologiam divinatricem (1493–94, published posthumously in 1496),1 his views on 13. the subject can be found in nearly all his texts. Pico’s comments on astrology 14. in his earlier writings and the development of his astrological views from 1486 15. until 1493 have been extensively studied, but modern scholars have not man- 16. aged to provide a convincing explanation for the apparent paradoxes within 17. Pico’s work. It seems that Pico endorses astrology in some of his writings and 18. condemns it in others. Most of Pico’s works were incomplete at the time of 19. his death and were subsequently published by his nephew, who promoted his 20. own intellectual agenda by constructing an idealised and somewhat distorted 21. representation of his uncle’s persona. As a result, it is hard to reconstruct the 22. development of Pico’s intellectual career. The present chapter’s main task 23. is to show the evolution of Pico’s philosophical outlook from 1486 to 1493, 24. the year when he started writing the Disputationes.2 The particular issue of 25. Giovanni Pico’s astrological views illustrates the development of his itinéraire 26. philosophique, which started with an early interest in Neoplatonic writings and 27. ambitious theological projects and ended with his later biblical commentaries. 28. 29. Pico obtained exceptional fame during his life. His enormous philosoph- 30. ical knowledge and the glory of being a polyglot attracted the attention of 31. Pico’s contemporaries, but at the same time, it has caused some misunder- 32. standings in secondary literature. In the first half of the twentieth century, 33. Pico was associated with all philosophical schools with which he had been 34. 35. 1 Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, Disputationes adversus astrologiam divinatricem, 2 vols, ed. 36. by Eugenio Garin (Turin: Aragno, 2004). Originally published in 1946–1952, this is the sole 37. modern edition of the Disputationes. 38. 39. 2 This chapter is an updated version of my article ‘Giovanni Pico della Mirandola and Astrology 40. (1486–1493): From Scientia naturalis to the Disputationes adversus astrologiam’, I Tatti Studies 41. in the Italian Renaissance, 21, 1 (2018), 47–66. 42. 43. Ovanes Akopyan - 978-90-04-44227-6 44. 45. 46. 47. 4 Chapter 1 48. 49. familiar. Thus, Bruno Nardi and Avery Dulles variously stress the Averroistic 50. and scholastic dimension of his work,3 whilst others describe Pico’s thought 51. as syncretistic, devoid of any original elements.4 Besides, in several scholarly 52. studies, Pico is sometimes referred to as a proponent of a new philosophy and 53. a reformer of Renaissance anthropology,5 following Giovanni Gentile’s rep- 54. resentation of Italian Renaissance philosophy.6 These views on Pico’s philos- 55. ophy are radically reconsidered, first in Eugenio Garin’s pioneering Giovanni 56. Pico della Mirandola: vita e dottrina7 and then in Fernand Roulier’s Jean Pic 57. de la Mirandole.
